mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P38 V8 engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only. A standard rebuild costs £2,000–£4,000, while a top-hat lined rebuild costs £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: No — the P38 V8 has a well-earned reputation for slipped cylinder liners, porous blocks and head gasket failure. The 4.0 is the most prone to liner slippage, followed by the 4.6. The root cause is an aluminium block with a 94mm bore that was essentially overbored from the original Buick design. The only permanent fix is a top-hat liner installation.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main issues are: (1) Slipped cylinder liners — the most devastating fault, causing pressurised coolant and overheating, (2) Overheating and cooling system failure — the root cause of liner slippage, and (3) Worn camshaft and timing chain — common on high-mileage examples.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £5,000–£12,000 P38, a £4,000–£5,500 recon is 30–110% of the car's value. For a clean example with good body and no corrosion, a top-hatted rebuild is a long-term investment that future-proofs the car. However, if the body or air suspension has significant issues, evaluate total restoration costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my P38 V8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. GEMS codes include 42D/44D (4.0) and 46D/48D (4.6). THOR codes include 57D/58D (4.0) and 59D/60D (4.6). The D refers to engine prefix — 42D = 4.0 High Compression, 46D = 4.6 High Compression, etc. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent slipped liners on the P38 V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prevention is not guaranteed, but you can significantly reduce the risk: (1) Keep the cooling system in perfect order — replace the radiator, thermostat, hoses and water pump preventatively, (2) Never ignore overheating — the first overheat does the damage, (3) Consider fitting a top-hat liner block — this is the only permanent solution, and (4) Use the correct coolant mix and change it regularly. Even with perfect maintenance, liner slippage remains a risk on later P38s.

replacementCosts.h2: Range Rover P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Budget priority; donor engine for rebuild
The only permanent fix for liner slippage

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a P38 V8 engine typically takes 15–25 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The engine bay is tight and body lift is often required for access. A standard rebuild costs £2,000–£4,000, while top-hat liners can triple the rebuild cost.

problems.title: Slipped Cylinder Liners (The Defining P38 V8 Fault)
problems.symptoms: Pressurised cooling system, coolant overflow from expansion tank, overheating, head gasket failure, rattling noise when engine is hot. Tapping noise from the block that disappears when cold.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — can occur under normal operating conditions on later P38s. 4.0 engines are most prone, followed by 4.6.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: overheating and pressurised coolant indicate imminent failure

problems.recommendation: This is the single most infamous fault on the P38 V8. The 94mm bore on an already-stressed aluminium block means the cylinder liners can move, allowing combustion gases to enter the cooling system. The only permanent fix is a top-hat liner installation — where the liner is machined with a lip that locks it into the block. Standard re-linering is cheaper but may not be a permanent solution. If you see a pressurised coolant system, bubbling in the expansion tank or any overheating, stop driving immediately. A £1,000–£1,200 re-liner is far cheaper than a £4,500–£7,000 replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full slipped liner guide → /failures/slipped-cylinder-liners/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Overheating & Cooling System Failure (Root Cause)
problems.symptoms: Temperature gauge rising, coolant loss with no visible leak, steam from engine bay, heater blowing cold, pressurised coolant system
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — cooling system components age and fail
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not typically progress to engine replacement if addressed early)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: overheating is what causes liner slippage

problems.recommendation: The P38 V8 runs hot — some HSE engines were deliberately mapped to run hotter for emissions compliance. The first overheat does the damage; after that, the damage causes more overheating. The most common causes are a clogged radiator, failed thermostat (which fails closed), water pump failure, or a split top radiator hose. If you're buying a P38, budget for a full cooling system refresh — radiator, thermostat, hoses and water pump. Keep the coolant system in perfect order and you dramatically reduce the risk of a slipped liner.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system guide

problems.title: Worn Camshaft & Timing Chain (High-Mileage)
problems.symptoms: Tappet noise from the top of the engine, loss of power, rough running, poor emissions
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not typically progress to engine replacement)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if tappet noise develops

problems.recommendation: Almost all used Rover V8 engines that have covered over 70,000 miles are suffering or beginning to suffer from worn camshaft, lifters and a stretched timing chain. If you're replacing the camshaft, you should also replace the hydraulic lifters, timing chain and sprockets, and check the rockers and rocker shafts. The timing cover should come off to check the oil pump gears. This is a well-understood job with plenty of specialist support.

failureLink.label: Read the full camshaft wear guide → /failures/camshaft-wear-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Re-linering is possible but may not be permanent
The only permanent fix — future-proofs the block
Viable if caught before liner damage occurs

buyingChecks.title: Buying This P38 V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Evidence of a top-hat lined replacement engine — this is the gold standard
* Full cooling system refresh (radiator, thermostat, hoses, water pump) recently completed
* No pressurisation of the cooling system — check by removing the coolant cap when cold
* Documented service history with regular oil changes
avoidIf: * Pressurised coolant system or bubbling in the expansion tank — this indicates liner failure
* Any history of overheating — the damage is often already done
* No cooling system maintenance history — this engine is unforgiving of neglect
* Tappet noise that hasn't been diagnosed — could indicate worn camshaft

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The P38 V8 is a genuine character — a Buick-derived aluminium V8 with a wonderful soundtrack and plenty of torque. But it's also one of the most notorious engines for catastrophic failure. The slipped liner issue is real, and the 4.0 is the most prone. The only way to own one with confidence is to buy an example with a top-hat lined engine already fitted, or budget for the work immediately. Without that, you're gambling on a £5,000+ engine replacement. Buy on documented history, not on optimism.

cta.label: → Compare P38 4.0 / 4.6 V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Range Rover P38 with the 4.0 or 4.6 V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant System — Check for pressurisation (remove cap when cold); look for bubbling; check for coolant loss
* Temperature Gauge — Does it sit steady? Any history of overheating?
* Cold Start — Listen for tappet noise or knocking from the top end
* Hot Start / Hot Running — Any rattling when hot? This can indicate slipped liners
* Service History — Including the V8 engine and confirmed cooling system maintenance
* Head Gasket Check — Look for oil in coolant or coolant in oil (mayonnaise on filler cap)
* Leaks — Check for oil leaks; they all leak a bit but excessive leaking indicates issues
* Top-Hat Evidence — Has the engine been top-hat lined? This is the single most valuable thing to find

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.

items.label: * Valvetrain
items.value: OHV, 2 valves per cylinder (16 valves total)

items.label: * THOR torque advantage
items.value: More low-end torque from long inlet runners
